sentences_input.text: I remember Ukrainians, and then they had a long ceremony of when they were kind of selection, when they were saying people who are something who has official documents and families of Jewish police, mainly families of Jewish police and said they can go out.
And indeed some people went out but not very many.

And after about maybe 40 minutes of sitting or kneeling-- I don't remember that precisely-- sitting I think on that hot pavement, we were then finally, they told us to get up and to-- and they formed us into a-- they formed us into a proper-- how to say-- proper line, proper marching group, about four people and everybody had to go with about four people in-- walking in fours.

And I remember that when I was there, when I was-- when we were waiting for them to finally to start taking us out of there, that I was then-- that it was a feeling I had then was of fear as I never had before in my life and never after.
That was quite amazing, and I-- and I knew-- I knew, I absolutely knew that that was the end, that there was no escape, that that was practically a death march.
And I was-- and I was praying as well in such an absolutely-- with so much feeling as never before and never after, I think.

And finally after about 40 minutes, they formed us into a march, and they opened the gate, which was a barbed wire gate at the end of Schultz area, and they started driving us straight ahead and then to the right toward the umschlagplatz.
